coredata.dc:title: Electrocatalytic performance of carbon dots/palladium nanoparticles composite towards hydrogen evolution reaction in acid medium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chandrasekaran, Pitchai
Jebakumar Immanuel Edison, Thomas Nesakumar
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) and nitrogen doped carbon dots supported palladium nanoparticles composite (n-Pd@NDCDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization and thermolytic reduction using Morinda citrifolia (M. citrifolia) fruit and palladium chloride as carbon and Pd precursors, respectively. The synthesized materials viz., n-Pd@NDCDs and NDCDs were duly characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The optical properties of NDCDs were studied by ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. Further, the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of n-Pd@NDCDs was evaluated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in 0.5 M aqueous H2SO4. The onset potential of n-Pd@NDCDs was about −0.195 VRHE, which was lower than NDCDS (−0.392 VRHE) and bare glassy carbon (−0.603 VRHE). The calculated Tafel slope values of n-Pd@NDCDs were 135 and 141.8 mV/dec, from the voltammetric and EIS methods, respectively. Moreover, the n-Pd@NDCDs exhibited small overpotential of 0.291 V to attain a current density of 10 mA/cm2. The EIS studies revealed that the HER charge transfer resistance was dropped from 84.3 to 18.3 Ω/cm2 while increasing of potential, which revealed good conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs. Thus the present work vouched for the candidature of n-Pd@NDCDs as an effective electrocatalyst for the HER in acidic medium.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen evolution reaction
Morinda citrifolia
Electrocatalyst
n-Pd@NDCDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NDCDs from M. citrifolia extract
Linear sweep voltammetry and Tafel slope measurements
Calculation of electrochemically active surface area

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of synthesized NDCDs at 20 nm scale (b-c) particle size distribution and SAED pattern for synthesized NDCDs.
(a–c) HR-TEM images for synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs at different magnifications.
XRD pattern for the synthesized NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs
FT-IR spectra of the synthesized NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs
XPS spectra of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs (a) deconvolution spectra of Pd 3d level (b) expanded spectra of carbon 1s (c) nitrogen 1s and (d) oxygen 1s level.
(a) Polarization curves for Bare GC, Bare Pt, NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs and (b) respective Tafel plots in 0.5 M H2SO4.
(a) Nyquist plots for Bare GC, and n-Pd@NDCDs/GC and (b) corresponding Bode plots.
(a) Nyquist plots of n-Pd@NDCDs/GC at different over potentials (b) and corresponding Bode plots.
Linear relation of HER overpotential and log (1/R3).
(a) Electrochemical active surface area estimated by cyclic voltammetry in 0.5 M H2SO4 and (b) linear fit of current density versus scan rate for n-Pd@NDCDs
Chronopotentiometry curve of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs at constant current density of −10 mA cm−2 for 1 h of hydrogen evolution.
EIS parameters of n-Pd@NDCDs in 0.5 M H2SO4 at different HER overpotentials.

head.ce:title: Electrocatalytic performance of carbon dots/palladium nanoparticles composite towards hydrogen evolution reaction in acid medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram, 624 302, Dindigul District, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, 38541, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan, 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) and nitrogen doped carbon dots supported palladium nanoparticles composite (n-Pd@NDCDs) were synthesized through hydrothermal carbonization and thermolytic reduction using Morinda citrifolia (M. citrifolia) fruit and palladium chloride as carbon and Pd precursors, respectively. The synthesized materials viz., n-Pd@NDCDs and NDCDs were duly characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The optical properties of NDCDs were studied by ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis), and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. Further, the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) performance of n-Pd@NDCDs was evaluated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in 0.5 M aqueous H2SO4. The onset potential of n-Pd@NDCDs was about −0.195 VRHE, which was lower than NDCDS (−0.392 VRHE) and bare glassy carbon (−0.603 VRHE). The calculated Tafel slope values of n-Pd@NDCDs were 135 and 141.8 mV/dec, from the voltammetric and EIS methods, respectively. Moreover, the n-Pd@NDCDs exhibited small overpotential of 0.291 V to attain a current density of 10 mA/cm2. The EIS studies revealed that the HER charge transfer resistance was dropped from 84.3 to 18.3 Ω/cm2 while increasing of potential, which revealed good conductivity and electrocatalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs. Thus the present work vouched for the candidature of n-Pd@NDCDs as an effective electrocatalyst for the HER in acidic medium.

ce:section.ce:para:
Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) is considered as a clean and green way for the production of hydrogen. It is recognized as an alternative to traditional energy [1]. Emission of CO (carbon monoxide) and CO2 (carbon dioxide) has become the major cause of climate change which leads to an increase in the global temperature. Further, the emission of sulfur from fossil fuels too leads to acid rain and sulfur fogs [2]. The excess energy received from the intermittent energy sources such as wind and sunlight could be stored in terms of hydrogen by water splitting [3–5]. Hydrogen gas produced by steam reforming and coal gasification also leads to CO2 emission. Hence, the electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction seems to be the unconventional approach for harvesting hydrogen [6].
(1)
2
H 3
O +
+ 2
e −
⇌
H 2
+ 2
H 2
O
E Cathode 0
= 0 V

The reaction occurs at the surface of the catalyst and the lowest overpotential (η) and higher current density are possible in the platinum (Pt) or its related materials. But Pt is highly expensive and not available easily. Generally, it lacks stability in high concentrated acidic condition of polymer electrolyte fuel cells which leads to decrease of H2 in large scale production. As per Sabatier's principle, Pt has the highest HER activity over other transition elements [7–9]. The Scientists explore the use of earth-abundant materials like Palladium (Pd), Molybdenum (Mo) and Tungsten (W) as the electrocatalysts for the water-splitting reactions such as oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and HER [10]. Najafpour et al. had reported that the manganese related compounds could be used as the photocatalyst for the water splitting reactions [11,12]. Recently Ming Nie et al. have discussed the palladium based MOF as the electrocatalyst for the HER [13] and Yuewei Li et al. reported molybdenum sulphide nanosheet and carbon nanotubes composites as the efficient electrocatalyst for the hydrogen evolution reaction [14]. In fact Palladium based materials are cheaper than their Pt counterparts and show tolerance to carbon monoxide (CO) [15]. The different types of carbon allotropes such as carbon dots, graphene, and carbon nanotubes (CNT) form composites with other materials, and act as catalysts for the HER. Carbon supported materials draw much attention in HER because of better durability in acid/base media and the catalysts are easily deposited on the high surface area of carbon materials (nano-sized) with myriad active sites. Also, carbon supports ensure the direction of electron transfer in recombination reactions [16,17]. In this study, palladium composite embedded with nitrogen doped carbon dots are used as the HER catalyst, where carbon dots act as supports for the palladium as well as a reducing agent for reducing the palladium (II) to zerovalent palladium.

HER is a multistep and reversible reaction (i.e. Eq. (1)) and HER takes place through three steps involving Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel steps. Based on these steps, HER may involve two plausible mechanisms one is Volmer-Tafel and another one is Volmer-Heyrovsky [18,19].

For the present study, Morinda citrifolia (M. citrifolia) was chosen as the carbon source for the green synthesis of carbon dots. M. citrifolia belongs to the family of Rubiaceae and it is an easily available edible plant. This fruit contains phytoconstituents such as coumarins, lignans, flavonoids, fattyacid glycosides, polysaccharides, anthraquinones, and carotenoids which are known carbon sources. The fruit juice has an antidiabetic activity, and agonistic activity against the P2Y receptor [20]. Further, the edible fruit comprises high content of potassium, which is responsible for its diuretic activity [21].

In this work, the hydrothermal carbonization process has been adopted for the preparation of nitrogen doped carbon dots (NDCDs) with M. citrifolia extract as carbon supply and aqueous ammonia as the nitrogen source. The palladium nanoparticles are synthesized using NDCDs and the resultant Pd nanomaterials supported by NDCDs is denoted as n-Pd@NDCDs. The synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs are characterized by regular investigative techniques. Moreover, the electrocatalytic HER performance of prepared n-Pd@NDCDs are explored by common electrochemical methods.

The M. citrifolia fruit was purchased from the local market. Aqueous NH3 (25%) and sulphuric acid (H2SO4) were procured from Central Drug House (CDH) and potassium ferricyanide purchased from Sigma-Aldrich was used. All electrolytic solutions were prepared with double distilled (DD) water. All the electrodes were purchased from Sinsil International Pvt. Ltd., Bengaluru, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NDCDs from M. citrifolia extract
ce:section.ce:para: 100 g of M. citrifolia fruit was washed and ground well with 50 mL of DD water, the resultant extract was filtered. In a typical hydrothermal process, 58 mL of extract and 2 mL of aq. NH3 were taken in 100 mL autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. Then it was cooled naturally and filtered by Whatmann No. 40 paper. The final brown colored solution was centrifuged to remove the larger particles and was kept in the refrigerator for future studies.

In a typical synthesis, 3.7 mL of 10 mM PdCl2 and 50 mL of NDCDs were mixed and refluxed at 100 °C for 8 h with constant stirring in an oil bath without any stabilizing agent. The color change from brown to the black clearly indicated the formation of Pd nanoparticles (n-Pd). Then it was centrifuged and washed several times to remove unbound carbon dots. The resultant material was denoted as the n-Pd@NDCDs.

The surface morphology of the synthesized catalyst was analyzed by the FEI Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN HR-TEM operating at 200 kV from Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, India. The elemental composition of synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs was analyzed using a BRUKER Nano, GmbH, D-12489 Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy with an accelerating voltage of 30 keV. The XRD pattern of the prepared catalyst was investigated by PANalytical X'Pert diffractometer equipment using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) operating at 45 kV and 40 mA. The Raman spectrum of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs was acquired using STR 500 nm focal length laser Raman spectrometer. The FT-IR spectrum was recorded over the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 using Jasco FT- IR 460 Plus. The electronic states present over n-Pd@NDCDs were analyzed using PHI 5000 Versa Probe II XPS analyzer.

Electrocatalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs towards HER was investigated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), Tafel and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4. The electrochemical measurements were carried out with Princeton Applied Research versa STAT 3 electrochemical workstation with a standard three electrode system. The glassy carbon (GC) (or) n-Pd@NDCDs/GC (or) NDCDs/GC (or) Pt sheet (1 cm2), Ag/AgCl, and Pt wire were used as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. In catalyst ink preparation, prepared material was dissolved in DD water, and 3 μL solution of it was coated on the GC electrode. The calculated loaded amount of n-Pd@NDCDs was about 0.05 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear sweep voltammetry and Tafel slope measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
The LSV measurement was carried out from 0 to −1.0 VRHE with the scan rate of 10 mV/s for all three electrodes. Based on Eq. (2), all the potentials were converted into a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) without iR drop compensation.
(2) E (vs.RHE) = E (vs. Ag/AgCl) + EAg/AgCl (reference) + 0.0591 V X pH (EAg/AgCl (ref) = 0.1976 V )
(3) η = a + b log |j|
The Tafel slope was aroused from Eq. (3) where η was the overpotential, b and j were Tafel slope and current density, respectively.
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies were made in the range of 105 to 0.1 Hz at 5 mV of amplitude with different bias potentials and the results were fitted through a proper circuit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of electrochemically active surface area
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was calculated by the double layer capacitance (Cdl) and it was determined by cyclic voltammetry measurements. The potential window of 0.45–0.73 VRHE was chosen for the cyclic voltammetric measurements at different scan rates from 10 to 100 mV/s in the non-faradic region where the electrode was completely polarized. From this measurement, the double layer capacitance was derived from the slope of average current density versus scan rate from the linear fit values and it was directly related to the ECSA. Eq. (4) shows the relation between double layer charging current density (jdl), scan rate (ν), and the double layer capacitance (Cdl).
(4) jdl = ν Cdl
(5) ECSA = Cdl / Cs
From Eq. (5), the ECSA was calculated using the double layer capacitance (Cdl) and Cs. where Cs is specific capacitance of the sample in identical electrolyte solution.

In the typical hydrothermal process, the photoluminescent NDCDs were synthesized from M. citrifolia fruit juice and it was visually noticed through the color change to brown. The n-Pd@NDCDs were synthesized from the NDCDs and PdCl2 and it was easily recognized by the color change from brown to black precipitate.

The surface morphology of the synthesized NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs were examined by the HR-TEM measurement. Fig. 1 (a–c) shows the HR-TEM images of NDCDs with particle size distribution and SAED pattern. The synthesized NDCDs were evenly distributed and spherical in nature. The average particle size of the NDCDs was 4.6 nm and the size distribution diameter ranged from 1 to 9 nm (Fig. 1b). The SAED pattern of NDCDs revealed the amorphous nature of synthesized NDCDs [22]. Fig. 2 (a–c) depicts the HR-TEM images of the prepared n-Pd@NDCDs at different magnifications. It clearly showed the porous nature and revealed the fact that carbon dots and palladium nanoparticles were interconnected [23]. Fig. S1 (supplementary information) displays the EDS spectrum of n-Pd@NDCDs, which revealed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and palladium.

Fig. 3 depicts the XRD pattern of the synthesized NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs. The prepared NDCDs showed broad Bragg diffraction peak at 22.7° for C (002) and d spacing value for this plane is 0.434 nm (calculated using the Bragg equation) which confirmed the amorphous nature of NDCDs [24]. The prepared n-Pd@NDCDs showed broad peak at 22.4° which correspond to the carbon dots (d value 0.44 nm) present in the n-Pd@NDCDs sample. The presence of four peaks at 40.6, 46.6, 66.65, and 82.2° correspond to the planes (111), (200), (220) and (311) of Pd [25]. The peaks at high angles (220) and (311) confirm the crystallinity of Pd nanoparticles. In addition to that, the major peaks at 28.7, 50.5, 58.9, and 73.9° were attributed to the potassium impurities, which arise from the biogenic source M. citrifolia fruit [26].

Using the Debye–Scherer Eq. (6), the average size of the palladium nanoparticles on the n-Pd@NDCDs composite was calculated to be ~20.86 nm.
(6)
B (2θ) = Kλ / L cos θ
where B is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity (FWHM) in radians, K is the dimensionless shape factor, typically the value of it is nearer to unity, λ is the incident X-ray wavelength in nanometer (0.154 nm), L is the average size of the crystalline domains [19,27]. XRD also confirmed the formation of sp2 carbons which are the intermediates in the formation of NDCDs involving such steps like polymerization, aromatization, and nucleation process [28].

Raman spectroscopy is the main tool for investigating the carbon-related materials. Fig. S2 shows the Raman spectrum of synthesized NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs. The relative intensity of defective region (D band) and graphitic region (G band) (ID/IG) of the prepared NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs were 0.74 and 0.59 respectively. The D band and G bands were correlated to sp3 defects in graphitic carbon and sp2 carbon atoms with E2g form respectively [29].

FT-IR spectroscopy is an important tool for analyzing the functional moieties present over the carbon dots surface. Fig. 4 depicts the FT-IR spectra of the prepared n-Pd@NDCDs and NDCDs. The synthesized NDCDs show bands at 3394, 2930, 1654, 1407, and 1124 cm−1 which correspond to the –OH/NH, C–H, C=O, COO−, and C–C stretching vibrations, respectively [30–32]. The prepared n-Pd@NDCDs exhibited bands at 3394, 2921, 1605, 1381, and 1117 cm−1 related to the OH/NH, C–H, COO−, C–N, and bending vibration modes of C–O vibrations correspondingly [33–36]. This confirms that the functional moieties such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl groups were present on the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs surface.

XPS is an important analytical tool for investigating the electronic state and oxidation state of the composite. The Pd 3d level of XPS generally shows doublets (3d5/2 and 3d3/2) because of the spin-orbit coupling. Fig. 5 (a–d) displays the high resolution XPS spectra of Pd 3d, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s. Fig. 5a shows the deconvolution spectrum of the palladium 3d level which shows two doublets. The intensive doublet (335.2 and 340.4 eV) may be assigned to metallic palladium and another doublet (337.1 and 343.2 eV) corresponds to the +2 oxidation state of palladium species [37,38]. These results show that 42% of Pd exists in a zero-valent oxidation state which confirmed the reducing ability of NDCDs.

Fig. 5b represents the expanded spectrum of C1s and showed two peaks at 284.8, and 286.75 eV which corresponds to the C–C and C=O states respectively [39,40]. Fig. 5c shows the deconvolution spectrum of the N1s level and showed two peaks at 396.3, and 397.47 eV which were related to the secondary amine (N–H) and amide (CO–NH2) groups respectively [41,42]. Fig. 5d shows the expanded spectrum of O1s and it shows two peaks at 529.25, 531.90 which were related to C–O–C and N–O/C–O groups respectively [43,44]. Thus the XPS of n-Pd@NDCDs revealed the existence of Pd, C, N, and O elements with organic functional phytoconstituents of M. citrifolia fruit.

The optical properties of synthesized NDCDs can be scrutinized by the UV–Visible and Fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. S3 shows the UV–Visible spectrum and depicts two typical (268 and 302 nm) shoulders which correspond to π- π∗and n- π∗ electronic transitions. The π- π∗and n- π∗transitions confirm the sp2 carbon (C=C) and carbonyl or amine groups present over the carbon dots [45]. Fig. S4a shows the photoluminescence spectrum of NDCDs which reveal the emission wavelength at 400 nm for different excitation wavelengths from 270 to 320 nm. The intensity of emission gradually decreased from 320 nm to 400 nm excitation wavelengths shown in Fig. S4b [46].

The electrocatalytic HER performance of n-Pd@NDCDs was investigated by LSV, Tafel, and EIS measurements in 0.5 M H2SO4 using a three electrode system at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. Fig. 6 (a and b) shows the LSV curves of bare Pt, n-Pd@NDCDs/GC, NDCDs/GC and bare GC, their corresponding Tafel slopes. From the LSV curves, electro kinetic parameters such as onset potential and overpotential at −10 mA cm−2 were computed. To be a good HER electrocatalyst, the material must have lower onset and overpotential than the bare GC and higher value than bare Pt [47,48]. From Fig. 6a, it could be seen that NDCDs and glassy carbon electrode showed an onset potential of −0.392 and −0.603 VRHE while n-Pd@NDCDs exhibited an onset potential of −0.195 VRHE. The bare Pt showed excelled HER activity with onset potential nearer to zero. This result clearly demonstrated the electrocatalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs.

The values of overpotential of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs, NDCDs, bare GC, and bare Pt electrodes at −10 mA cm−2 were found to be −0.291, −0.478,-0.728 and −0.089 VRHE respectively. These results confirmed the excellent electrocatalytic behavior of n-Pd@NDCDs.

It is well known that Tafel slope is a vital parameter to predict the electrocatalytic property of the test material. It is observed that HER kinetics is faster for materials with smaller Tafel slope. Fig. 6b shows linear part of Tafel slopes for n-Pd@NDCDs, NDCDs, bare GC, and bare Pt. The calculated values of Tafel slope for n-Pd@NDCDs was 135 mV/dec, while that of NDCDs, bare GC and bare Pt were found to be 175, 234 and 39 mV/dec, respectively. A.P. Murthy et al. had stated that a Tafel slope of 120 mV/dec arises when volmer step becomes the rate determining step [18,49]. The present value of 135 mV/dec closer to the value reported by A.P. Murthy et al. vouches for the Volmer step being the rate determining step. Hence it can be suggested that n-Pd@NDCDs follow Volmer-Tafel mechanism wherein the discharge of protons occurs first and those on the surface of the catalyst the hydrogen coverage increases. As the rate of discharge of protons increases, the rate of recombination of H2 also increases and the H2 evolution becomes more [50]. Thus the Tafel slope study throws light on the mechanistic pathway of HER.

EIS is the foremost important tool for scrutinizing the coating effect on the electrode surface, which gives the clear information of electrode kinetics in the electrolyte [51]. In order to study the coating effect, the impedance study was performed for bare GC and n-Pd@NDCDs/GC at the OCP of 0.545 and 0.775 VRHE, respectively. Fig. 7 shows the Nyquist and Bode plots of bare GC and n-Pd@NDCDs/GC in 0.5 M aqueous H2SO4. The phase angle Bode plot of bare GC exhibits one time constant at 93 Hz, attributed to the mass transfer controlled ion diffusion reaction between GC surface and electrolyte whereas, n-Pd@NDCDs/GC shows two time constants at 144 and 0.1 Hz, credited to the coating effect and mass transfer controlled reaction, respectively. Therefore, two different equivalent circuit models (Inset of Fig. 7) were used for the fitting of experimental results. The bare GC consists of three elements such as R1 (solution resistance), R2 (diffusion resistance) and Q2 (capacitance of charge/mass controlled ion diffusion reaction with surface linearity factor), and the n-Pd@NDCDs/GC includes five elements comprising of R1 (solution resistance), R2 (coating resistance), Q2 (coating capacitance), R3 (charge/mass transfer resistance) and Q3 (capacitance of charge/mass transfer reaction). The fitted results were almost overlaid to the experimentally acquired spectra and the obtained total resistance (R1+R2) of bare GC (4.36 × 105 Ω/cm2) was higher than that of the total resistance (R1+R2+R3) of n-Pd@NDCDs/GC (1.3 × 104 Ω/cm2), which revealed the high electrical conductivity of n-Pd@NDCDs [52,53].

Further, the electrocatalytic HER performance of n-Pd@NDCDs was evaluated at different potentials viz., −0.10, −0.115, −0.13, −0.145, −0.16, −0.175 and −0.19 VRHE by EIS method. Fig. 8 depicts the Nyquist and Bode plots of n-Pd@NDCDs/GC in 0.5 M H2SO4. The phase angle Bode plots of n-Pd@NDCDs/GC represent three time constants at high frequency (10,000 Hz), mid frequency (2 Hz) and low frequency (0.1 Hz), which endorsed the coating effect, HER charge transfer and hydrophobicity of GC electrode surface, respectively, which were clearly seen in the Nyquist plots as two depressed semicircles and a slanting line at high, mid and low frequency regions. Moreover, the experimentally acquired EIS data exactly fitted with the proposed equivalent circuit model shown in Fig. 8a (Inset), where, R1, R2, R3, R4 were the solution resistance, coating resistance, HER charge transfer resistance, and hydrophobic surface resistance of GC, respectively, Q2 and Q3 represent coating capacitance and constant phase element of double layer capacitance respectively. The fitted results were presented in Table 1 . From the results, a notable drop in charge transfer resistance (R3) was observed from 84.3 to 18.3 Ω/cm2, while increasing of potential from −0.10 to −0.19 VRHE. Similarly, the hydrophobic surface resistance of GC was also decreased from 100.3 to 2 Ω/cm2 (−0.10 to −0.19 VRHE). Thus the results implied that, the total resistance was drastically decreased and the HER kinetics became faster at high negative potential [8]. Generally, the total resistance is inversely proportional to the total capacitances (Q2 and Q3). It is confirmed that the n-Pd@NDCDs lowers the resistance of GC by increasing the contribution of capacitance [54]. In addition, the charge transfer resistance (R3) values, were utilized to get the Tafel slope.

Fig. 9 shows the linear relation of log (1/R3) and potential values, which yields a straight line with a Tafel slope of −141.8 mV/dec. The Tafel slope of n-Pd@NDCDs obtained from voltammetric (−135 mV/dec) and impedance methods correlate well. Thus, EIS method of Tafel slope revealed the pure charge transfer kinetics of HER [55].

It is well known that the electrocatalytic performance of a material highly depends on the double layer capacitance in terms of ECSA. The ECSA of n-Pd@NDCDs was estimated using cyclic voltammetry at electrochemical double layer area with various scan rates. Fig. 10 shows the CV graphs and scan rate vs. current density graph of n-Pd@NDCDs. Using CV results, the calculated double layer capacitance is about 0.060 mF/cm2. Further, the ECSA of n-Pd@NDCDs is calculated using the equation ECSA = Cdl/Cs, where Cdl is the double layer capacitance of n-Pd@NDCDs and Cs is the specific capacitance of the catalyst (0.040 mF/cm2) [56,57]. The obtained ECSA of n-Pd@NDCDs is 1.5 cm2, which reduce the charge transfer resistance and promote the HER kinetics.

A chronopotentiometry experiment was used to evaluate the stability of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs for 1 h at constant current density of −10 mA/cm2 (Fig. 11 ). It shows the minor shift of potential from −0.2820 to −02832 VRHE. The results confirmed that the good stability and high catalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs towards HER in aqueous H2SO4. The excellent electrocatalytic activity of n-Pd@NDCDs was attributed to the doping of nitrogen atom, which modifies the electronic charge of the neighboring carbon atoms and increased the electronegativity, which improved the catalytic activity [58].

In summary, we have demonstrated the hydrothermal mediated synthesis of NDCDs and n-Pd@NDCDs composite. The FT-IR and XPS studies revealed the active functional groups like carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups which were functionalized on the n-Pd@NDCDs. The Raman and XRD results imply that the prepared n-Pd@NDCDs were moderately graphitic. The electrocatalytic activity towards HER was tested in 0.5 M H2SO4 and the onset potential of synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs/GC was −0.195 VRHE, which was lower than the NDCDs (−0.392 VRHE). The Tafel slopes of synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs and NDCDs were about 135 and 175 mV/dec, respectively. The impedance results revealed that the charge transfer resistance value of the synthesized n-Pd@NDCDs/GC was lower than the bare GC. Thus the electrochemical results exhibited that the M. citrifolia derived NDCDs supported Pd composite (n-Pd@NDCDs) showed good electrocatalytic activity towards HER. The as-prepared n-Pd@NDCDs can be used successfully as the electrocatalyst for hydrogen production for the future demand.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), India for the financial support for this work through the NET-JRF Scheme (Award No: 09/715(0020)/2019-EMR-I dated on July 02, 2019).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2020.07.262.
